controls.value: the field empties; eighths regroup in twos
the ground gives way: one line, two mouths, no bass
capture as union: both subjects at once, home key
hard-tongued hunting call; let the lilt spring
the Hare: light, darting, off the beat
the scent recovered: Hound head as a signal
augmentation round: the Hound at double length, bell tones
THE STOP: one figure, then the piece's only silence
the seal: stop-echo in bare octaves, bright to the cutoff

sections.name: Hound Exposition
journey: one unaccompanied hunting call becomes a five-voice field
destination: the full quintet locks for the first time at bars 17-20

harmony: Bb major; entries oscillate tonic and dominant only
process: horn states the Hound alone; bassoon answers tonally (5->1 head) while the horn carries the countersubject

placements.realization: the Hound alone from silence: the piece begins as one unaccompanied hunting call
tonal answer at F2: the bass arrives; head bends 1->5 to 5->1
CS in dominant form (down a 4th, E naturals as the pull to F) against the answer; syncope chain, barline breaths

harmony: Bb/F alternation continues; no outside keys
process: clarinet subject over bassoon countersubject; oboe answer over clarinet countersubject; horn takes a composed breath, bassoon becomes a real fugal bass

placements.realization: third entry, subject at the clarinet octave, entering from silence

CS in the bassoon, recomposed as ONE afterbeat bell chain: every attack on the shared syncope phase (third eighth of each dotted beat), 1.5-length bells tolling in 3rds and 6ths against the subject stations with short eighth tails at the last slot, a breath at every barline; over the stream bar the bells thin to a struck eighth then two long tones; the close works the dominant-pull A twice, the last A resolving to Bb ON the cadence beat, landing with the subject's own attack

the horn falls silent through the clarinet's head-call, then SNAP-ECHOES it launched ON the beat (the echo lands its third note on the shared afterbeat slot, striking together with the bassoon's bell), leans a tied F across the 9/10 barline that releases exactly as the bassoon's next bell strikes, then joins the bassoon D bell in unison under the climb (the two-voice afterbeat choir) and breathes out into the bars 11-12 tacet
fourth entry: the answer returns higher, oboe at F4, from silence

CS in the clarinet (dominant form), the SAME afterbeat bell chain as the bassoon's setting - every attack on the shared syncope phase, barline breaths, eighth tails at the last slot - opening with the kept E-against-Bb dominant tritone resolving by step to F, a single long F bell over the answer's stream bar, and the close working E to F to A, the last bell landing ON the cadence beat with the answer's own F

the fugal bass as the BEAT stratum proper: every kick lands ON a dotted beat (roots and thirds of the local step - F-C-F-A, F-A-F with the C3 lift, A-A-Bb-F under the stream, F-D then the rising snap into the cadence), each hit ringing an eighth into a shared rest; the snap-dotted turn (.75+.25+.5) is the Hound's own call cell launched on the mid-bar beat; the bar-16 F holds under the answer's cadence and hands the register to the crown

harmony: Bb arrival at 20 undercut by the episode pickup
process: flute crowns the exposition; first full five-voice texture; arrival immediately tips into the episode

placements.realization: fifth entry crowns the exposition from above; Bb5 crest at bar 18

CS in tonic form under the flute, on the one shared afterbeat phase throughout: the bar-18 bells track the subject's climb (C-D-D) instead of striking against its passing tones, the F lean now sits on the true 18/19 seam slot and dissolves into the horn's F as the stream begins, the long C bell rings over the stream bar; bar-20 keeps its G-A rise and the Bb-A-G undercut eighths so the arrival still tips into the episode

KICK CHOIR, upper voice (crown BEAT stratum): every attack shares the bassoon's slot exactly - downbeat, mid-bar, and pre-cadence kicks in bars 17-18 and 20, the full four-beat tread under the stream bar - voiced a 6th/10th above the bassoon on the local chord tones, rising Bb-D-D-F with the stream and closing on the D of the arrival triad

LEANING WEIGHT, the crown's ONE hanging voice: every entry on the afterbeat slot, long re-struck D leans framing a 5th under the countersubject's A bells and hung across the 17/18 and 19/20 barlines, the F4 lean taking over the oboe's released F pitch as the stream bar begins, and the last lean rising to F inside the arrival's Bb triad - a slow underswell in one phase with the bell layer

KICK CHOIR, floor voice (crown BEAT stratum): downbeat root kicks, the Hound's own repeated-pitch snap cell (.75+.25+.5) launched on the mid-bar beat of 17 and 18 under the subject's long notes, the full four-beat D-Bb-Bb-F tread beneath the stream bar in rhythmic unison with the clarinet, and the F-to-Bb3 root close at the arrival (low Bb register stays reserved for bar 96)

sections.name: First Chase Episode
journey: the field empties and tilts toward the minor
destination: the sequence lands on D, dominant of G minor

harmony: Bb -> Cm -> D-of-Gm by sequence; modulation lives in the episode
process: two-to-three-voice sequence built from the Hound tail and countersubject fragment; hemiola regroups the eighths in twos; flute and horn rest

placements.realization: the sequence heads as SIGNALS: the old stream cell compressed into snap figures (.75+.25 from the Hound's call) that speak and stop - two calls a bar with real silence between, sequenced down a step; then the first hemiola bar kept: quarter chains regroup the eighths in twos

the answers across distance: quick sixteenth flicks (four-note neighbor turns) flashing back in the snaps' gaps, stepping down A-G-G-F with the sequence; clarinet then breathes through the hemiola bar

the floor kicks instead of walking: one hit per dotted beat, then a gap - the tread heard THROUGH the rests; the D-G-C-F circle-of-fifths bar keeps its pitches under the hemiola, now punctured to the same kick language

roles exchanged after the hemiola: the clarinet takes the SNAP signals low, the A of bar 24 shading to Ab at bar 25 - the same call darkening as the field tilts to C minor, every call landing into silence
the flick answers migrate above the snaps (the dialogue inverted): sixteenth turns stepping F-Eb-Eb-D-D-C down the sequence, each one flashed into a gap and gone

kicked floor through the C-minor tilt: Ab arrives in the bass as a hit-gap-hit tread (Fm color at the bar-26 third beat), the last A natural kick consonant under the oboe's falling turn and pivoting the field toward D
second hemiola: falling-third quarter chains against the clarinet lilt (the licensed friction bar)
the friction voice re-enters speaking SNAP: two snap cells on the dotted beats against the hemiola pairs, then the held F - dotted-beat grouping sharpened, not smoothed
the kick tread holds the true meter under the friction - same pitches, heard through gaps - then the Eb-D semitone catch: the sequence caught on D
the drive as urgent signalling: two snaps, then a sixteenth wobble that trips onto the F sharp - the leading tone of G minor arrived at through panic, held, then the half-beat lift
the upper voice answers snap for snap, converging by step onto D; both voices lift for the last half beat - a shared breath before the landing call

harmony: D major as V of G minor
process: the sequence is caught and held; a half-bar breath before the Hare

placements.realization: the D floor as kicks through gaps - an articulated pedal, never a drone - then the bass joins the final snap-call itself
the ECHO: the clarinet answers each oboe call a dotted beat later and lower, then falls to the held third under the last chord
THE LANDING AS A CALL: snap-call on D (the Hound's own snap on the new dominant), echoed by the clarinet, then one final unison-D snap opening to the full triad - held to mid-bar, then the breath

sections.name: Hare Exposition
journey: a second character in the minor; the Hound loses the scent (horn tacet throughout)
destination: G minor half-close at 46, left open

harmony: G minor; answer in D MINOR per the minor-answer rule
process: flute states the Hare (running class, off-beat launch); oboe answers in D minor under the flute's adapted countersubject

placements.realization: the Hare in G minor, fresh after ten bars' rest: off-beat spring into the bar-30 silence, wiggle, D5 crest, F# bite, lilt flip at bar 33

the floor still speaks only at beat 1 (contract G9) but the bell is now the Hound's own SNAP (.75+.25+.5) confined to the first dotted beat and swallowed by silence - the tread as a dotted kick heard through rests, and the first hint that the Hound is still out there while the Hare runs
the Hare answered in D MINOR (minor-answer rule), C sharp as leading tone; degree-mapped from the G-minor form

CS.r with the TIED-LEAN profile, one profile held for the whole entry: every bell on the shared afterbeat phase and held long, leaning on the minor's C and Bb over the D-minor answer; bar 37 rings the harmonic-minor rise Bb-C#-D in octave with the answer's own lilt tones, then the C lean hangs ACROSS the 37/38 barline onto the answer's A; the close re-strikes A twice and resolves C#-D landing ON the cadence beat with the answer's own D; the bar-35 head note still anchors the downbeat the off-beat answer leaves open

process: clarinet entry, then the bassoon takes the Hare as the bass arrives; horn silent all section

placements.realization: third entry: the Hare back in G minor an octave under the flute's statement; the clarinet carries the bass function until the bassoon arrives

CS.r with the PUNCTURED-BELL profile, one profile for the whole entry: short bells only, each struck on the shared afterbeat phase and falling into a rest - F rising to the Bb bell then closing G-F# in bar 39, and in bars 40-41 the chain thins to single bells so its strikes ALTERNATE strictly with the offstage Hound's D-echoes (bell - echo - bell); bar 42 tolls the repeated D bell and ends on F# releasing into the bass entry's G, the approach carved by rest

the flute re-strikes its cadence D once to anchor the entry's downbeat, then turns SNAP-ECHO: the Hound's snap-call cell on a single repeated D (the offstage Hound baying on the dominant), each call launched ON a beat and answering the oboe's last bell in strict alternation - bar 40's call in the Hare's post-crest fall, bar 41's in the gap over the lilt's long F sharp; tacet bar 42 keeps the thin approach to the bass entry
fourth entry: the bass arrives with the Hare; the tail is bent in bar 46 (A-G-E-D instead of the tonic close) so the statement lands on D, the open half-close

CS.r with the LEAN-AT-MIDPOINT profile: punctured afterbeat bells circling G/F sharp through bars 43-44 (the G bell still anchors the bar-43 downbeat the off-beat entry leaves open), then the single D lean hung ACROSS the 44/45 barline at the statement's midpoint, tied onto the Hare's own low D; bells resume F#-G-A rising through 45, and the close strikes E then A ON the cadence beat so the bar-46 half-close vertical stays an open fifth, no third

fragment leakage toward S4 in the CHASE dialect: the sixteenth dart now LAUNCHES on the shared afterbeat slot, springing together with the clarinet's midpoint lean and the bass's D (its adjudicated rising G-A-Bb-C shape into the stab kept); the crest hung as a leaning bell from the bar-45 downbeat, the quiet Bb-Bb-A snap answer tracking the bass's own Bb-A, and the bar-46 D5 bell still held through the open half-close

the offstage Hound again: the same quiet repeated-D snap-echo as bars 40-41 dropped over the bass statement's F-sharp bar - the far-away call has been circling D all section, and the half-close lands ON that D; the flute then rests bar 46 entirely, a composed breath before it leads the bar-47 relay

sections.name: Open Chase
journey: nobody owns the texture; fragments of both subjects relay and brush past
destination: F locked as dominant preparation at 57-58

harmony: G minor -> Eb (evaded to the sixth chord) -> C minor approached and evaded to Ab
process: Hound-head and Hare-head fragments relayed mid-phrase between adjacent voices: each fragment is begun by one player and finished by the next in the same register; bassoon floor walks the evasions and exits on a hanging leading tone

placements.realization: the Hare's wiggle extended into a full-beat sixteenth SCRAMBLE - six notes of panic - then the flute is SEIZED: a sixteenth stab on the crest and silence
the oboe attacks the abandoned fragment OUT OF THE GAP (relay 1): the Hare tail re-spoken as two Hound snaps, F sharp biting
Hound head-call on Eb (the G-minor dominant evaded to the sixth chord) tightened to a DOUBLE SNAP - snap on the root, snap on the fifth - then dropped mid-call
flute finishes the Hound fragment out of the gap (relay 2): the arch tail as a snap, then a dart flick curling back to Bb
the wiggle scramble on Eb major, pressed harder - six sixteenths and the oboe is seized on the crest stab, mid-phrase
clarinet rips the fragment out of the gap (relay 3): the tail as paired snaps, the D-Eb-F turn now a snapped kick
the hunting call minorized AND snapped: double snap 1-5 on C minor over the deceptive Ab bass, then dropped
oboe completes the minor call out of the gap (relay 4) and its dart flick lands on D over the hanging B natural - both subjects have brushed past in adjacent voices without combining
the floor kicks every evasion through gaps - G minor ground, the Eb sixth chord, C minor, the deceptive Ab - and exits on a hanging B natural kick, unresolved, as the bass vanishes for the window

harmony: C minor, unrooted; the bass is silent for three bars
process: one running line split between flute and oboe in three-eighth alternation (hocket); the line descends, bottoms out on the leading tone, and turns to rise; clarinet adds the B-natural lilt; the bassoon re-enters at 54 resolving its hanging B to C

placements.realization: hocket, first mouth, now JAGGED: the flute's halves of the ripped line alternate snap cells and sixteenth darts instead of even eighths - same line skeleton (falls to the leading tone at 52, turns, rises, funnels at 54), each fragment torn off into a rest

hocket, second mouth: the answering halves attack out of the gaps the flute leaves - dart where the flute snapped, snap where it darted, so the seam is audible as a RIP; bottoms out on B natural twice, the second one hanging into bar 55
the lilt returns as a leading-tone BITE: C-B-C snap cells gnawing under the rising hocket, then falling Eb-D-C and Ab-G-F snaps as the dominant gathers - each bite spat into a gap

the window closes with kicks, not a walk: the bassoon re-enters on C (resolving the B natural hung at bar 50), drops to Ab, re-strikes C, and lands G - dominant of the coming sequence step, each hit through a gap

harmony: C minor -> D minor -> F locked as dominant of Bb
process: E3: episode 1's model inverted with parts swapped - the cell now rises in the low voice, the gap answers fall from above; the horn re-enters with the Hound head as a signal; hemiola drive-bars 57-58 lock onto F and flow into the bar-59 downbeat

placements.realization: THE SIGNAL: the horn's first notes since bar 20 - the Hound head-call (1-5 leap, repeated notes, minor-third tail) alone in the bass register, tail bent to D toward the next sequence step

E3 = E1's snap signals INVERTED: the falling snap cells of bars 21-26 now RISE (.75+.25 head climbing through its own third), two calls a bar into silence, sequenced UP a step per bar (C, then D - the A natural pivoting toward F)
E3 answers with PARTS SWAPPED: E1's flick answers ran below the cell; here they FALL from above as sixteenth turns, split between flute and oboe
the second answer mouth flicks in the gaps the flute leaves - the hocket's ripped-line energy carried into the episode; the last G hands straight to the bar-57 drive
the floor under the D step: the falling D-C-Bb-G tread kicked through gaps, gathering the dominant approach

hemiola drive (the span's only hemiola, per the ledger): falling quarter-pairs on the locked F, the E.model's hemiola form; bar 58 ends 6/4-to-5/3 - the last quarter is clipped to an eighth so the flute takes a real breath (the lift into the downbeat) before its Hare statement at 59; the A octave bassoon/oboe carries the final pull to Bb
parallel sixths under the flute quarters; bar 58 carries the cadential 6/4 (Bb4) resolving to A - the leading tone aimed at bar 59

SIXTEENTH PRESSURE inside the hemiola: the clarinet fires a dart scramble on every second dotted beat against the quarter-pairs - the S2 friction voice at chase gear, short slurred bursts with real breaths, anchored to the locked F
the Hound's repeated-note call fused with the hemiola: ten hammered quarter-note F's, then a breath before the horn takes the subject at 59
F locked: re-struck dotted-quarter F's holding the true meter under the cross-grouping; the last eighth walks F-A toward Bb - the seam drives through into bar 59, nothing closes

sections.name: Combination
journey: capture as union: the two subjects prove they are one music
destination: formal destination at 59-62: first simultaneous complete statements, home key

harmony: Bb major restored; the return IS the union
process: PAIR statement 1 (Hare above, horn Hound, countersubject free voice); then the pairing inverted -- the bassoon takes the Hound, the orientation audibly flipped

placements.realization: THE DESTINATION: the Hound complete at Bb3 (PAIR.spec statement 1) while the Hare runs above - the first time in the piece both subjects sound at once; the horn drives in off its composed breath
the Hare complete at the C5-F5 core (PAIR.spec statement 1), its D5 launch taking the bar-58 C5 by step; bar 61 flips to lilt while the Hound streams; both land the final Bb together - the union seal

CS.line as the free third voice, an octave below its lab register (the S1 bassoon registration, verified invertible at the octave), and at the union it LEANS instead of breathing: the bar-end C is held over the barline (a prepared second against the Hound's arch head, rising to D), and the F leans into the stream bar - the syncope chain becomes a suspension chain for this one statement; the bar-4 barline breath keeps the identity; the clarinet holds its bar-58 F common tone into the A3 syncope

the union floor as DOTTED KICKS through rests: root hit, gap, chord kick, gap, then a snap-dotted turn (.75+.25) at mid-bar and real silence for the last dotted beat of every bar - the Hound's own snap rhythm under its own statement; stations kept (Bb homecoming, G-minor lean under the crown, Eb-F dominant close) and the long gap before bar 63 still prepares the bassoon's Hound entry
the pairing INVERTED: the Hound on top at Bb4 (PAIR.spec orientation 2), the oboe's first notes since bar 58 - the register exchange with the Hare is two octaves, unmissable
the bass BECOMES the subject: the Hound at Bb2 doubling the oboe in double octaves; against the Hare above it these are the statement-1 interval classes, so both verified orientations sound at once
the Hare an octave down at the C4 core (PAIR.spec orientation 2), running BELOW the Hound between the two octave copies

CS.line at its lab register, now UNDER the inverted Hound (the octave-inversion license) - the syncope bells ring inside the double-octave frame, and the first bar-end C leans over the barline as a prepared second rising to D (answering the clarinet's suspension in the first pairing); the later barline breaths keep the identity; horn tacet: a composed breath between its subject and the crest

harmony: Bb with brief G-minor shading; strong close at 70
process: the crest: flute melodic high point of the piece over all five in distinct value classes; then a two-bar liquidation drains to a thread

placements.realization: the CREST: the Hound's head and arch re-derived as a climb - the bar-2 arch bent upward through G5 and C6 to D6, the piece's highest pitch, landing ON the bar-69 downbeat and HELD two dotted beats mid-phrase (G-minor shading under the peak), then falling through the V6/5 to the strongest close before the end

the Hare's wiggle as an unbroken stream under the crest - the combined element: while the flute holds the D6 peak the oboe keeps the chase running, rising C5-F5 into the cadence, then a composed breath before it leads the liquidation

LEANING CHAIN under the crest: weak-beat entries held across the dotted beats (the F hung through half of bar 67, the G tied over the 68/69 barline into the G-minor peak), then the dominant SEVENTH taken by step (F-Eb) at bar 69 and hung three full beats before resolving to D at the bar-70 downbeat - the suspension language of the pairing statements extended into the crest

HANGING WEIGHT: the horn re-enters off the beat and hangs one F across the whole 67/68 barline (six beats of tenuto weight), leans a G over the 68/69 seam INTO the G-minor peak, carries the dominant's third (A) under the turn, and lays F-D under the cadence - the accompaniment as suspended mass while the crest holds

the crest floor KICKS the same harmonic path it used to walk: tonic hits through gaps, the G-vi and Eb-IV stations kicked into the peak bar, the dominant hammered as a snap-dotted F under the turn, and the F-Bb root fall still earning the strongest cadence before the end - tread through rests while the horn hangs and the clarinet leans

LIQUIDATION AS LEANING: the head-call's tones stretched into tenuto sighs - Bb hung over the first two dotted beats, the falling D tied ACROSS the 71/72 barline, then the fragment halved (Bb-F) and gone; nothing kicks, everything hangs

the answering thread, staggered a dotted beat behind the oboe at the Bb3 register its stretto entry will claim: leaning tenutos that overlap the oboe's sighs, the F tied ACROSS the 71/72 barline, one last low D, then four beats of composed silence - the breath out of which the bar-73 stretto entry springs

sections.name: Stretto Drive
journey: the pack in full cry: the Hound overlaps itself at tightening distances
destination: dominant lock, arrival withheld into the stop

harmony: Bb/F harmony only; close stretto does not modulate
process: three entries at two bars' distance (clarinet, oboe, flute); earlier entries break off, the last completes

placements.realization: stretto entry 1 at Bb3, grown straight out of the liquidation's register: head and arch, then the voice breaks off into free counterpoint as the next entry arrives

the break-off as SEIZURE: the entry's tail ripped into snap fragments with a gap after each - hit, silence, kick - then a rising sixteenth dart snatches the voice up to F and it is gone (the full-bar breath at 77)
stretto entry 2, an octave above the clarinet's while its predecessor is still mid-phrase: the pack begins to overlap itself

the second break-off cut harder: one snap and the voice is gone for a half bar under the flute's entry, one low kick out of the silence; then the syncope bell rings once, a gap, and a rising dart kicks toward its own bar-79 entry
stretto entry 3 runs COMPLETE (the contract's last-entry-completes), its stream and tag crossing the span seam into bars 79-80 while the one-bar stretto piles up beneath it

head-calls in the Hound's snap punctuating between entries - the snap, a composed GAP, then the kick, so each answering F lands like an accent; answering the clarinet's arch, saluting the oboe's, then the snap on F hammering Bb then the F kick into its own bar-80 entry; real breaths between every call
the floor holds Bb/F ground under the piling entries - triad walks with a half-bar breath before the flute's entry, closing with the D3 step and the F2 kick-pickup that becomes the bar-79 pedal
back from its breath ATTACKING OUT OF THE GAP: a snap, a wobbling dart, and the voice converges on D4 with a lift before its own displaced entry at 81

process: four entries at one bar's distance including the horn, one per arsin et thesin; bassoon re-strikes the F pedal in the subject's lilt rhythm; all five converge rhythmically at 83-84

placements.realization: one-bar stretto, entry 1: the oboe drives in over the fresh F pedal while the flute's statement streams above - two limbs of the Hound at once
one-bar stretto, entry 2: THE HORN in the pack at last - the piece's first voice rejoining its own subject mid-pile, one bar behind the oboe
break-off into a RISING bell line (A-C-D) over the horn's climb, marking the clarinet's displaced entry beneath, then a full-bar breath before the lock
the PER ARSIN ET THESIN entry: the Hound displaced to the weak half-bar (bar 81 beat 4), beats still mapping to beats per the stretto table - the pack's stride breaks its own bar-line
entry 4, half a bar behind the clarinet's: the tightest spacing of the piece - the head-call alone before the whole pack converges
the F pedal with ATTACK LOGIC: re-struck in the subject's own head-call and lilt rhythms, octave F3 kicks, never held - compressing to hammered eighths and the collective breath at the end of 84

the DOMINANT LOCK: the drive caught on F major, the flute hammering the A-natural leading tone at the top; written-out compression into rhythmic-unison eighths, arrival WITHHELD - the last eighth is the whole quintet's breath before the stop
lock, parallel thirds under the flute: the E natural biting in the unison-rhythm drive
lock: the clarinet abandons its arch mid-phrase (the compression eats the last entry) and hammers the dominant third
lock: horn fifths at the bottom, then the octave vault to F4 for the unison-rhythm eighths, CONTRARY to the falling flute - maximum pressure, no cadence, everything aimed at the bar-85 unison attack

sections.name: Stop And Final Round
journey: one hard stop, then the proclamation: the Hound at double length under the running Hare
destination: final authentic cadence; last bar bare octaves on Bb

harmony: F harmony, one figure, then silence
process: the piece's only full-texture break: all five hit one shared rhythmic figure and stop dead

placements.realization: F.stop_cell: the Hound's SNAP CALL itself (.75+.25+.5) plus one hit, on bare F octaves (OT9 stop adapted); the hit cut off at 2.5 and the rest of the bar genuinely empty - the piece's only full-texture silence
F.stop_cell, unison with the flute
F.stop_cell at the middle octave
F.stop_cell, the horn hard-tongued at the bottom of its call register
F.stop_cell on the pedal F it has hammered since 79: the pedal's last word is the stop itself

process: the Hound in augmentation (horn + bassoon in octaves) under the Hare running in flute + clarinet, oboe free descant; the widest spread of the piece opens at 90-93

placements.realization: H.subject COMPLETE in augmentation, all four bars at double values: the same tune at double length, bell tones, nothing decorated - the proclamation the whole piece aims at

the augmented Hound doubled at the octave below; the doubling DROPS for the final Bb (bar 93 second half) so no sustained low Bb sounds before the reserved bar-96 seal, and the breath sets up the cadence call
the Hare STILL RUNNING: R.subject bars 1-2 (Bb form, the lab-verified augmentation pairing) off the beat over the bell tones; the flute then breathes two bars, handing the tune on mid-statement
R.subject bars 3-4 at the flute's own register: the DODGE leap Bb4->F5 passes BETWEEN the players at the barline (flute ends 87 on Bb4, clarinet springs to F5) - one Hare, two mouths, real breathing

the descant as LONG TIED ARCS: single tenuto tones hung across the 86/87, 87/88 and 88/89 barlines (Bb-A over the first seam, the rising Bb lean over the second, Bb over the third), CS syncope heritage in the off-beat attack points; the line rises to C6 with the round's widest ring then falls G-F as it hands the top register to the flute's coming climb

the WIDEST SPREAD opens and the Hare hits its PANIC GEAR: the wiggle rebuilt a fifth higher with sixteenth darts, then off a half-bar breath a sixteenth scramble F-G-A-Bb springs the dodge leap Bb5->F6 onto the bar-92 downbeat - the piece's highest note, held, above the bar-69 D6 crest - and the running close lands the union Bb WITH the augmented Hound's final bell

the Hare still running below the climb: wiggle answer traded against the flute's breaths, its own dodge leap Bb4->F5 at the 92/93 barline, and the final running close in DECLARED OCTAVES with the flute - two Hare voices sealing onto Bb together

the descant FALLS as the flute climbs: tied arcs stepping down through the 91/92 barline (F hung over the seam under the flute's F6 spring), sinking to a Bb4 lean while the highest note of the piece rings above, then rising one last D-F-D to settle on the warm third inside the final Bb ring

process: a rhythmic cadence, not a held-chord recital; the last bar is bare octaves on Bb

placements.realization: V.final_cadence rest-accented: the horn throws the Hound head-call one last time (the voice that opened the piece calls it home), then the V7 drive becomes an EXCHANGE - low pair snaps the leading tone out of the bar-95 downbeat, falls silent while the trio answers, snaps again, and only the last dotted beat converges in eighths before the lift into the seal

head-call with the horn, dominant root drive, then the RESERVED LOW Bb SPENT: Bb1 for the first time in the piece, the root fall F2->Bb1 sealing the cadence at the very bottom; the dominant drive is snapped calls out of gaps with the horn, converging in eighths only at the last dotted beat

the answered call (1->5 on the dominant), the V7 drive ANSWERED out of the low pair's gap at the reclaimed F6 (snap, silence, snap), and the final spring F6->Bb6: the last note is the highest note of the piece, flute at the top of the bare octaves
answer-call rising 3->1 in the dominant, then the fifth of the V7 spoken in the trio's gap-answers, stepping C6->Bb5 into the seal

answer-call bending 5->1 downward (the tonal-answer gesture compressed), then the SEVENTH of the V7 flashed in the trio's gap-answers - the one dissonant tone in the exchange, licensed cadential pressure - falling into the octave Bb
the Hound alone from silence: the piece begins as one unaccompanied hunting call
tonal answer at F2: the bass arrives; head bends 1->5 to 5->1
CS in dominant form (down a 4th, E naturals as the pull to F) against the answer; syncope chain, barline breaths
third entry, subject at the clarinet octave, entering from silence

CS in the bassoon, recomposed as ONE afterbeat bell chain: every attack on the shared syncope phase (third eighth of each dotted beat), 1.5-length bells tolling in 3rds and 6ths against the subject stations with short eighth tails at the last slot, a breath at every barline; over the stream bar the bells thin to a struck eighth then two long tones; the close works the dominant-pull A twice, the last A resolving to Bb ON the cadence beat, landing with the subject's own attack

the horn falls silent through the clarinet's head-call, then SNAP-ECHOES it launched ON the beat (the echo lands its third note on the shared afterbeat slot, striking together with the bassoon's bell), leans a tied F across the 9/10 barline that releases exactly as the bassoon's next bell strikes, then joins the bassoon D bell in unison under the climb (the two-voice afterbeat choir) and breathes out into the bars 11-12 tacet
fourth entry: the answer returns higher, oboe at F4, from silence

CS in the clarinet (dominant form), the SAME afterbeat bell chain as the bassoon's setting - every attack on the shared syncope phase, barline breaths, eighth tails at the last slot - opening with the kept E-against-Bb dominant tritone resolving by step to F, a single long F bell over the answer's stream bar, and the close working E to F to A, the last bell landing ON the cadence beat with the answer's own F

the fugal bass as the BEAT stratum proper: every kick lands ON a dotted beat (roots and thirds of the local step - F-C-F-A, F-A-F with the C3 lift, A-A-Bb-F under the stream, F-D then the rising snap into the cadence), each hit ringing an eighth into a shared rest; the snap-dotted turn (.75+.25+.5) is the Hound's own call cell launched on the mid-bar beat; the bar-16 F holds under the answer's cadence and hands the register to the crown
fifth entry crowns the exposition from above; Bb5 crest at bar 18

CS in tonic form under the flute, on the one shared afterbeat phase throughout: the bar-18 bells track the subject's climb (C-D-D) instead of striking against its passing tones, the F lean now sits on the true 18/19 seam slot and dissolves into the horn's F as the stream begins, the long C bell rings over the stream bar; bar-20 keeps its G-A rise and the Bb-A-G undercut eighths so the arrival still tips into the episode

KICK CHOIR, upper voice (crown BEAT stratum): every attack shares the bassoon's slot exactly - downbeat, mid-bar, and pre-cadence kicks in bars 17-18 and 20, the full four-beat tread under the stream bar - voiced a 6th/10th above the bassoon on the local chord tones, rising Bb-D-D-F with the stream and closing on the D of the arrival triad

LEANING WEIGHT, the crown's ONE hanging voice: every entry on the afterbeat slot, long re-struck D leans framing a 5th under the countersubject's A bells and hung across the 17/18 and 19/20 barlines, the F4 lean taking over the oboe's released F pitch as the stream bar begins, and the last lean rising to F inside the arrival's Bb triad - a slow underswell in one phase with the bell layer

KICK CHOIR, floor voice (crown BEAT stratum): downbeat root kicks, the Hound's own repeated-pitch snap cell (.75+.25+.5) launched on the mid-bar beat of 17 and 18 under the subject's long notes, the full four-beat D-Bb-Bb-F tread beneath the stream bar in rhythmic unison with the clarinet, and the F-to-Bb3 root close at the arrival (low Bb register stays reserved for bar 96)

the sequence heads as SIGNALS: the old stream cell compressed into snap figures (.75+.25 from the Hound's call) that speak and stop - two calls a bar with real silence between, sequenced down a step; then the first hemiola bar kept: quarter chains regroup the eighths in twos

the answers across distance: quick sixteenth flicks (four-note neighbor turns) flashing back in the snaps' gaps, stepping down A-G-G-F with the sequence; clarinet then breathes through the hemiola bar

the floor kicks instead of walking: one hit per dotted beat, then a gap - the tread heard THROUGH the rests; the D-G-C-F circle-of-fifths bar keeps its pitches under the hemiola, now punctured to the same kick language

roles exchanged after the hemiola: the clarinet takes the SNAP signals low, the A of bar 24 shading to Ab at bar 25 - the same call darkening as the field tilts to C minor, every call landing into silence
the flick answers migrate above the snaps (the dialogue inverted): sixteenth turns stepping F-Eb-Eb-D-D-C down the sequence, each one flashed into a gap and gone

kicked floor through the C-minor tilt: Ab arrives in the bass as a hit-gap-hit tread (Fm color at the bar-26 third beat), the last A natural kick consonant under the oboe's falling turn and pivoting the field toward D
second hemiola: falling-third quarter chains against the clarinet lilt (the licensed friction bar)
the friction voice re-enters speaking SNAP: two snap cells on the dotted beats against the hemiola pairs, then the held F - dotted-beat grouping sharpened, not smoothed
the kick tread holds the true meter under the friction - same pitches, heard through gaps - then the Eb-D semitone catch: the sequence caught on D
the drive as urgent signalling: two snaps, then a sixteenth wobble that trips onto the F sharp - the leading tone of G minor arrived at through panic, held, then the half-beat lift
the upper voice answers snap for snap, converging by step onto D; both voices lift for the last half beat - a shared breath before the landing call
the D floor as kicks through gaps - an articulated pedal, never a drone - then the bass joins the final snap-call itself
the ECHO: the clarinet answers each oboe call a dotted beat later and lower, then falls to the held third under the last chord
THE LANDING AS A CALL: snap-call on D (the Hound's own snap on the new dominant), echoed by the clarinet, then one final unison-D snap opening to the full triad - held to mid-bar, then the breath
the Hare in G minor, fresh after ten bars' rest: off-beat spring into the bar-30 silence, wiggle, D5 crest, F# bite, lilt flip at bar 33

the floor still speaks only at beat 1 (contract G9) but the bell is now the Hound's own SNAP (.75+.25+.5) confined to the first dotted beat and swallowed by silence - the tread as a dotted kick heard through rests, and the first hint that the Hound is still out there while the Hare runs
the Hare answered in D MINOR (minor-answer rule), C sharp as leading tone; degree-mapped from the G-minor form

CS.r with the TIED-LEAN profile, one profile held for the whole entry: every bell on the shared afterbeat phase and held long, leaning on the minor's C and Bb over the D-minor answer; bar 37 rings the harmonic-minor rise Bb-C#-D in octave with the answer's own lilt tones, then the C lean hangs ACROSS the 37/38 barline onto the answer's A; the close re-strikes A twice and resolves C#-D landing ON the cadence beat with the answer's own D; the bar-35 head note still anchors the downbeat the off-beat answer leaves open
third entry: the Hare back in G minor an octave under the flute's statement; the clarinet carries the bass function until the bassoon arrives

CS.r with the PUNCTURED-BELL profile, one profile for the whole entry: short bells only, each struck on the shared afterbeat phase and falling into a rest - F rising to the Bb bell then closing G-F# in bar 39, and in bars 40-41 the chain thins to single bells so its strikes ALTERNATE strictly with the offstage Hound's D-echoes (bell - echo - bell); bar 42 tolls the repeated D bell and ends on F# releasing into the bass entry's G, the approach carved by rest

the flute re-strikes its cadence D once to anchor the entry's downbeat, then turns SNAP-ECHO: the Hound's snap-call cell on a single repeated D (the offstage Hound baying on the dominant), each call launched ON a beat and answering the oboe's last bell in strict alternation - bar 40's call in the Hare's post-crest fall, bar 41's in the gap over the lilt's long F sharp; tacet bar 42 keeps the thin approach to the bass entry
fourth entry: the bass arrives with the Hare; the tail is bent in bar 46 (A-G-E-D instead of the tonic close) so the statement lands on D, the open half-close

CS.r with the LEAN-AT-MIDPOINT profile: punctured afterbeat bells circling G/F sharp through bars 43-44 (the G bell still anchors the bar-43 downbeat the off-beat entry leaves open), then the single D lean hung ACROSS the 44/45 barline at the statement's midpoint, tied onto the Hare's own low D; bells resume F#-G-A rising through 45, and the close strikes E then A ON the cadence beat so the bar-46 half-close vertical stays an open fifth, no third

fragment leakage toward S4 in the CHASE dialect: the sixteenth dart now LAUNCHES on the shared afterbeat slot, springing together with the clarinet's midpoint lean and the bass's D (its adjudicated rising G-A-Bb-C shape into the stab kept); the crest hung as a leaning bell from the bar-45 downbeat, the quiet Bb-Bb-A snap answer tracking the bass's own Bb-A, and the bar-46 D5 bell still held through the open half-close

the offstage Hound again: the same quiet repeated-D snap-echo as bars 40-41 dropped over the bass statement's F-sharp bar - the far-away call has been circling D all section, and the half-close lands ON that D; the flute then rests bar 46 entirely, a composed breath before it leads the bar-47 relay
the Hare's wiggle extended into a full-beat sixteenth SCRAMBLE - six notes of panic - then the flute is SEIZED: a sixteenth stab on the crest and silence
the oboe attacks the abandoned fragment OUT OF THE GAP (relay 1): the Hare tail re-spoken as two Hound snaps, F sharp biting
Hound head-call on Eb (the G-minor dominant evaded to the sixth chord) tightened to a DOUBLE SNAP - snap on the root, snap on the fifth - then dropped mid-call
flute finishes the Hound fragment out of the gap (relay 2): the arch tail as a snap, then a dart flick curling back to Bb
the wiggle scramble on Eb major, pressed harder - six sixteenths and the oboe is seized on the crest stab, mid-phrase
clarinet rips the fragment out of the gap (relay 3): the tail as paired snaps, the D-Eb-F turn now a snapped kick
the hunting call minorized AND snapped: double snap 1-5 on C minor over the deceptive Ab bass, then dropped
oboe completes the minor call out of the gap (relay 4) and its dart flick lands on D over the hanging B natural - both subjects have brushed past in adjacent voices without combining
the floor kicks every evasion through gaps - G minor ground, the Eb sixth chord, C minor, the deceptive Ab - and exits on a hanging B natural kick, unresolved, as the bass vanishes for the window

hocket, first mouth, now JAGGED: the flute's halves of the ripped line alternate snap cells and sixteenth darts instead of even eighths - same line skeleton (falls to the leading tone at 52, turns, rises, funnels at 54), each fragment torn off into a rest

hocket, second mouth: the answering halves attack out of the gaps the flute leaves - dart where the flute snapped, snap where it darted, so the seam is audible as a RIP; bottoms out on B natural twice, the second one hanging into bar 55
the lilt returns as a leading-tone BITE: C-B-C snap cells gnawing under the rising hocket, then falling Eb-D-C and Ab-G-F snaps as the dominant gathers - each bite spat into a gap

the window closes with kicks, not a walk: the bassoon re-enters on C (resolving the B natural hung at bar 50), drops to Ab, re-strikes C, and lands G - dominant of the coming sequence step, each hit through a gap
THE SIGNAL: the horn's first notes since bar 20 - the Hound head-call (1-5 leap, repeated notes, minor-third tail) alone in the bass register, tail bent to D toward the next sequence step

E3 = E1's snap signals INVERTED: the falling snap cells of bars 21-26 now RISE (.75+.25 head climbing through its own third), two calls a bar into silence, sequenced UP a step per bar (C, then D - the A natural pivoting toward F)
E3 answers with PARTS SWAPPED: E1's flick answers ran below the cell; here they FALL from above as sixteenth turns, split between flute and oboe
the second answer mouth flicks in the gaps the flute leaves - the hocket's ripped-line energy carried into the episode; the last G hands straight to the bar-57 drive
the floor under the D step: the falling D-C-Bb-G tread kicked through gaps, gathering the dominant approach

hemiola drive (the span's only hemiola, per the ledger): falling quarter-pairs on the locked F, the E.model's hemiola form; bar 58 ends 6/4-to-5/3 - the last quarter is clipped to an eighth so the flute takes a real breath (the lift into the downbeat) before its Hare statement at 59; the A octave bassoon/oboe carries the final pull to Bb
parallel sixths under the flute quarters; bar 58 carries the cadential 6/4 (Bb4) resolving to A - the leading tone aimed at bar 59

SIXTEENTH PRESSURE inside the hemiola: the clarinet fires a dart scramble on every second dotted beat against the quarter-pairs - the S2 friction voice at chase gear, short slurred bursts with real breaths, anchored to the locked F
the Hound's repeated-note call fused with the hemiola: ten hammered quarter-note F's, then a breath before the horn takes the subject at 59
F locked: re-struck dotted-quarter F's holding the true meter under the cross-grouping; the last eighth walks F-A toward Bb - the seam drives through into bar 59, nothing closes
THE DESTINATION: the Hound complete at Bb3 (PAIR.spec statement 1) while the Hare runs above - the first time in the piece both subjects sound at once; the horn drives in off its composed breath
the Hare complete at the C5-F5 core (PAIR.spec statement 1), its D5 launch taking the bar-58 C5 by step; bar 61 flips to lilt while the Hound streams; both land the final Bb together - the union seal

CS.line as the free third voice, an octave below its lab register (the S1 bassoon registration, verified invertible at the octave), and at the union it LEANS instead of breathing: the bar-end C is held over the barline (a prepared second against the Hound's arch head, rising to D), and the F leans into the stream bar - the syncope chain becomes a suspension chain for this one statement; the bar-4 barline breath keeps the identity; the clarinet holds its bar-58 F common tone into the A3 syncope

the union floor as DOTTED KICKS through rests: root hit, gap, chord kick, gap, then a snap-dotted turn (.75+.25) at mid-bar and real silence for the last dotted beat of every bar - the Hound's own snap rhythm under its own statement; stations kept (Bb homecoming, G-minor lean under the crown, Eb-F dominant close) and the long gap before bar 63 still prepares the bassoon's Hound entry
the pairing INVERTED: the Hound on top at Bb4 (PAIR.spec orientation 2), the oboe's first notes since bar 58 - the register exchange with the Hare is two octaves, unmissable
the bass BECOMES the subject: the Hound at Bb2 doubling the oboe in double octaves; against the Hare above it these are the statement-1 interval classes, so both verified orientations sound at once
the Hare an octave down at the C4 core (PAIR.spec orientation 2), running BELOW the Hound between the two octave copies

CS.line at its lab register, now UNDER the inverted Hound (the octave-inversion license) - the syncope bells ring inside the double-octave frame, and the first bar-end C leans over the barline as a prepared second rising to D (answering the clarinet's suspension in the first pairing); the later barline breaths keep the identity; horn tacet: a composed breath between its subject and the crest

the CREST: the Hound's head and arch re-derived as a climb - the bar-2 arch bent upward through G5 and C6 to D6, the piece's highest pitch, landing ON the bar-69 downbeat and HELD two dotted beats mid-phrase (G-minor shading under the peak), then falling through the V6/5 to the strongest close before the end

the Hare's wiggle as an unbroken stream under the crest - the combined element: while the flute holds the D6 peak the oboe keeps the chase running, rising C5-F5 into the cadence, then a composed breath before it leads the liquidation

LEANING CHAIN under the crest: weak-beat entries held across the dotted beats (the F hung through half of bar 67, the G tied over the 68/69 barline into the G-minor peak), then the dominant SEVENTH taken by step (F-Eb) at bar 69 and hung three full beats before resolving to D at the bar-70 downbeat - the suspension language of the pairing statements extended into the crest

HANGING WEIGHT: the horn re-enters off the beat and hangs one F across the whole 67/68 barline (six beats of tenuto weight), leans a G over the 68/69 seam INTO the G-minor peak, carries the dominant's third (A) under the turn, and lays F-D under the cadence - the accompaniment as suspended mass while the crest holds

the crest floor KICKS the same harmonic path it used to walk: tonic hits through gaps, the G-vi and Eb-IV stations kicked into the peak bar, the dominant hammered as a snap-dotted F under the turn, and the F-Bb root fall still earning the strongest cadence before the end - tread through rests while the horn hangs and the clarinet leans

LIQUIDATION AS LEANING: the head-call's tones stretched into tenuto sighs - Bb hung over the first two dotted beats, the falling D tied ACROSS the 71/72 barline, then the fragment halved (Bb-F) and gone; nothing kicks, everything hangs

the answering thread, staggered a dotted beat behind the oboe at the Bb3 register its stretto entry will claim: leaning tenutos that overlap the oboe's sighs, the F tied ACROSS the 71/72 barline, one last low D, then four beats of composed silence - the breath out of which the bar-73 stretto entry springs
stretto entry 1 at Bb3, grown straight out of the liquidation's register: head and arch, then the voice breaks off into free counterpoint as the next entry arrives

the break-off as SEIZURE: the entry's tail ripped into snap fragments with a gap after each - hit, silence, kick - then a rising sixteenth dart snatches the voice up to F and it is gone (the full-bar breath at 77)
stretto entry 2, an octave above the clarinet's while its predecessor is still mid-phrase: the pack begins to overlap itself

the second break-off cut harder: one snap and the voice is gone for a half bar under the flute's entry, one low kick out of the silence; then the syncope bell rings once, a gap, and a rising dart kicks toward its own bar-79 entry
stretto entry 3 runs COMPLETE (the contract's last-entry-completes), its stream and tag crossing the span seam into bars 79-80 while the one-bar stretto piles up beneath it

head-calls in the Hound's snap punctuating between entries - the snap, a composed GAP, then the kick, so each answering F lands like an accent; answering the clarinet's arch, saluting the oboe's, then the snap on F hammering Bb then the F kick into its own bar-80 entry; real breaths between every call
the floor holds Bb/F ground under the piling entries - triad walks with a half-bar breath before the flute's entry, closing with the D3 step and the F2 kick-pickup that becomes the bar-79 pedal
back from its breath ATTACKING OUT OF THE GAP: a snap, a wobbling dart, and the voice converges on D4 with a lift before its own displaced entry at 81
one-bar stretto, entry 1: the oboe drives in over the fresh F pedal while the flute's statement streams above - two limbs of the Hound at once
one-bar stretto, entry 2: THE HORN in the pack at last - the piece's first voice rejoining its own subject mid-pile, one bar behind the oboe
break-off into a RISING bell line (A-C-D) over the horn's climb, marking the clarinet's displaced entry beneath, then a full-bar breath before the lock
the PER ARSIN ET THESIN entry: the Hound displaced to the weak half-bar (bar 81 beat 4), beats still mapping to beats per the stretto table - the pack's stride breaks its own bar-line
entry 4, half a bar behind the clarinet's: the tightest spacing of the piece - the head-call alone before the whole pack converges
the F pedal with ATTACK LOGIC: re-struck in the subject's own head-call and lilt rhythms, octave F3 kicks, never held - compressing to hammered eighths and the collective breath at the end of 84

the DOMINANT LOCK: the drive caught on F major, the flute hammering the A-natural leading tone at the top; written-out compression into rhythmic-unison eighths, arrival WITHHELD - the last eighth is the whole quintet's breath before the stop
lock, parallel thirds under the flute: the E natural biting in the unison-rhythm drive
lock: the clarinet abandons its arch mid-phrase (the compression eats the last entry) and hammers the dominant third
lock: horn fifths at the bottom, then the octave vault to F4 for the unison-rhythm eighths, CONTRARY to the falling flute - maximum pressure, no cadence, everything aimed at the bar-85 unison attack

F.stop_cell: the Hound's SNAP CALL itself (.75+.25+.5) plus one hit, on bare F octaves (OT9 stop adapted); the hit cut off at 2.5 and the rest of the bar genuinely empty - the piece's only full-texture silence
F.stop_cell, unison with the flute
F.stop_cell at the middle octave
F.stop_cell, the horn hard-tongued at the bottom of its call register
F.stop_cell on the pedal F it has hammered since 79: the pedal's last word is the stop itself
H.subject COMPLETE in augmentation, all four bars at double values: the same tune at double length, bell tones, nothing decorated - the proclamation the whole piece aims at

the augmented Hound doubled at the octave below; the doubling DROPS for the final Bb (bar 93 second half) so no sustained low Bb sounds before the reserved bar-96 seal, and the breath sets up the cadence call
the Hare STILL RUNNING: R.subject bars 1-2 (Bb form, the lab-verified augmentation pairing) off the beat over the bell tones; the flute then breathes two bars, handing the tune on mid-statement
R.subject bars 3-4 at the flute's own register: the DODGE leap Bb4->F5 passes BETWEEN the players at the barline (flute ends 87 on Bb4, clarinet springs to F5) - one Hare, two mouths, real breathing

the descant as LONG TIED ARCS: single tenuto tones hung across the 86/87, 87/88 and 88/89 barlines (Bb-A over the first seam, the rising Bb lean over the second, Bb over the third), CS syncope heritage in the off-beat attack points; the line rises to C6 with the round's widest ring then falls G-F as it hands the top register to the flute's coming climb

the WIDEST SPREAD opens and the Hare hits its PANIC GEAR: the wiggle rebuilt a fifth higher with sixteenth darts, then off a half-bar breath a sixteenth scramble F-G-A-Bb springs the dodge leap Bb5->F6 onto the bar-92 downbeat - the piece's highest note, held, above the bar-69 D6 crest - and the running close lands the union Bb WITH the augmented Hound's final bell

the Hare still running below the climb: wiggle answer traded against the flute's breaths, its own dodge leap Bb4->F5 at the 92/93 barline, and the final running close in DECLARED OCTAVES with the flute - two Hare voices sealing onto Bb together

the descant FALLS as the flute climbs: tied arcs stepping down through the 91/92 barline (F hung over the seam under the flute's F6 spring), sinking to a Bb4 lean while the highest note of the piece rings above, then rising one last D-F-D to settle on the warm third inside the final Bb ring

V.final_cadence rest-accented: the horn throws the Hound head-call one last time (the voice that opened the piece calls it home), then the V7 drive becomes an EXCHANGE - low pair snaps the leading tone out of the bar-95 downbeat, falls silent while the trio answers, snaps again, and only the last dotted beat converges in eighths before the lift into the seal

head-call with the horn, dominant root drive, then the RESERVED LOW Bb SPENT: Bb1 for the first time in the piece, the root fall F2->Bb1 sealing the cadence at the very bottom; the dominant drive is snapped calls out of gaps with the horn, converging in eighths only at the last dotted beat

the answered call (1->5 on the dominant), the V7 drive ANSWERED out of the low pair's gap at the reclaimed F6 (snap, silence, snap), and the final spring F6->Bb6: the last note is the highest note of the piece, flute at the top of the bare octaves
answer-call rising 3->1 in the dominant, then the fifth of the V7 spoken in the trio's gap-answers, stepping C6->Bb5 into the seal

answer-call bending 5->1 downward (the tonal-answer gesture compressed), then the SEVENTH of the V7 flashed in the trio's gap-answers - the one dissonant tone in the exchange, licensed cadential pressure - falling into the octave Bb

CS in the bassoon, recomposed as ONE afterbeat bell chain: every attack on the shared syncope phase (third eighth of each dotted beat), 1.5-length bells tolling in 3rds and 6ths against the subject stations with short eighth tails at the last slot, a breath at every barline; over the stream bar the bells thin to a struck eighth then two long tones; the close works the dominant-pull A twice, the last A resolving to Bb ON the cadence beat, landing with the subject's own attack

the horn falls silent through the clarinet's head-call, then SNAP-ECHOES it launched ON the beat (the echo lands its third note on the shared afterbeat slot, striking together with the bassoon's bell), leans a tied F across the 9/10 barline that releases exactly as the bassoon's next bell strikes, then joins the bassoon D bell in unison under the climb (the two-voice afterbeat choir) and breathes out into the bars 11-12 tacet

the union floor as DOTTED KICKS through rests: root hit, gap, chord kick, gap, then a snap-dotted turn (.75+.25) at mid-bar and real silence for the last dotted beat of every bar - the Hound's own snap rhythm under its own statement; stations kept (Bb homecoming, G-minor lean under the crown, Eb-F dominant close) and the long gap before bar 63 still prepares the bassoon's Hound entry

CS.line as the free third voice, an octave below its lab register (the S1 bassoon registration, verified invertible at the octave), and at the union it LEANS instead of breathing: the bar-end C is held over the barline (a prepared second against the Hound's arch head, rising to D), and the F leans into the stream bar - the syncope chain becomes a suspension chain for this one statement; the bar-4 barline breath keeps the identity; the clarinet holds its bar-58 F common tone into the A3 syncope
the Hare complete at the C5-F5 core (PAIR.spec statement 1), its D5 launch taking the bar-58 C5 by step; bar 61 flips to lilt while the Hound streams; both land the final Bb together - the union seal
THE DESTINATION: the Hound complete at Bb3 (PAIR.spec statement 1) while the Hare runs above - the first time in the piece both subjects sound at once; the horn drives in off its composed breath

CS.line as the free third voice, an octave below its lab register (the S1 bassoon registration, verified invertible at the octave), and at the union it LEANS instead of breathing: the bar-end C is held over the barline (a prepared second against the Hound's arch head, rising to D), and the F leans into the stream bar - the syncope chain becomes a suspension chain for this one statement; the bar-4 barline breath keeps the identity; the clarinet holds its bar-58 F common tone into the A3 syncope

the bass BECOMES the subject: the Hound at Bb2 doubling the oboe in double octaves; against the Hare above it these are the statement-1 interval classes, so both verified orientations sound at once
the Hare an octave down at the C4 core (PAIR.spec orientation 2), running BELOW the Hound between the two octave copies

the crest floor KICKS the same harmonic path it used to walk: tonic hits through gaps, the G-vi and Eb-IV stations kicked into the peak bar, the dominant hammered as a snap-dotted F under the turn, and the F-Bb root fall still earning the strongest cadence before the end - tread through rests while the horn hangs and the clarinet leans

LEANING CHAIN under the crest: weak-beat entries held across the dotted beats (the F hung through half of bar 67, the G tied over the 68/69 barline into the G-minor peak), then the dominant SEVENTH taken by step (F-Eb) at bar 69 and hung three full beats before resolving to D at the bar-70 downbeat - the suspension language of the pairing statements extended into the crest

the CREST: the Hound's head and arch re-derived as a climb - the bar-2 arch bent upward through G5 and C6 to D6, the piece's highest pitch, landing ON the bar-69 downbeat and HELD two dotted beats mid-phrase (G-minor shading under the peak), then falling through the V6/5 to the strongest close before the end

HANGING WEIGHT: the horn re-enters off the beat and hangs one F across the whole 67/68 barline (six beats of tenuto weight), leans a G over the 68/69 seam INTO the G-minor peak, carries the dominant's third (A) under the turn, and lays F-D under the cadence - the accompaniment as suspended mass while the crest holds

the Hare's wiggle as an unbroken stream under the crest - the combined element: while the flute holds the D6 peak the oboe keeps the chase running, rising C5-F5 into the cadence, then a composed breath before it leads the liquidation

the crest floor KICKS the same harmonic path it used to walk: tonic hits through gaps, the G-vi and Eb-IV stations kicked into the peak bar, the dominant hammered as a snap-dotted F under the turn, and the F-Bb root fall still earning the strongest cadence before the end - tread through rests while the horn hangs and the clarinet leans

the CREST: the Hound's head and arch re-derived as a climb - the bar-2 arch bent upward through G5 and C6 to D6, the piece's highest pitch, landing ON the bar-69 downbeat and HELD two dotted beats mid-phrase (G-minor shading under the peak), then falling through the V6/5 to the strongest close before the end

LEANING CHAIN under the crest: weak-beat entries held across the dotted beats (the F hung through half of bar 67, the G tied over the 68/69 barline into the G-minor peak), then the dominant SEVENTH taken by step (F-Eb) at bar 69 and hung three full beats before resolving to D at the bar-70 downbeat - the suspension language of the pairing statements extended into the crest

the CREST: the Hound's head and arch re-derived as a climb - the bar-2 arch bent upward through G5 and C6 to D6, the piece's highest pitch, landing ON the bar-69 downbeat and HELD two dotted beats mid-phrase (G-minor shading under the peak), then falling through the V6/5 to the strongest close before the end

HANGING WEIGHT: the horn re-enters off the beat and hangs one F across the whole 67/68 barline (six beats of tenuto weight), leans a G over the 68/69 seam INTO the G-minor peak, carries the dominant's third (A) under the turn, and lays F-D under the cadence - the accompaniment as suspended mass while the crest holds

the answering thread, staggered a dotted beat behind the oboe at the Bb3 register its stretto entry will claim: leaning tenutos that overlap the oboe's sighs, the F tied ACROSS the 71/72 barline, one last low D, then four beats of composed silence - the breath out of which the bar-73 stretto entry springs

LIQUIDATION AS LEANING: the head-call's tones stretched into tenuto sighs - Bb hung over the first two dotted beats, the falling D tied ACROSS the 71/72 barline, then the fragment halved (Bb-F) and gone; nothing kicks, everything hangs

lock: the clarinet abandons its arch mid-phrase (the compression eats the last entry) and hammers the dominant third

the DOMINANT LOCK: the drive caught on F major, the flute hammering the A-natural leading tone at the top; written-out compression into rhythmic-unison eighths, arrival WITHHELD - the last eighth is the whole quintet's breath before the stop
lock: horn fifths at the bottom, then the octave vault to F4 for the unison-rhythm eighths, CONTRARY to the falling flute - maximum pressure, no cadence, everything aimed at the bar-85 unison attack
lock, parallel thirds under the flute: the E natural biting in the unison-rhythm drive

lock, parallel thirds under the flute: the E natural biting in the unison-rhythm drive
the F pedal with ATTACK LOGIC: re-struck in the subject's own head-call and lilt rhythms, octave F3 kicks, never held - compressing to hammered eighths and the collective breath at the end of 84
lock: the clarinet abandons its arch mid-phrase (the compression eats the last entry) and hammers the dominant third

F.stop_cell, unison with the flute

the augmented Hound doubled at the octave below; the doubling DROPS for the final Bb (bar 93 second half) so no sustained low Bb sounds before the reserved bar-96 seal, and the breath sets up the cadence call
the Hare STILL RUNNING: R.subject bars 1-2 (Bb form, the lab-verified augmentation pairing) off the beat over the bell tones; the flute then breathes two bars, handing the tune on mid-statement
H.subject COMPLETE in augmentation, all four bars at double values: the same tune at double length, bell tones, nothing decorated - the proclamation the whole piece aims at

the descant as LONG TIED ARCS: single tenuto tones hung across the 86/87, 87/88 and 88/89 barlines (Bb-A over the first seam, the rising Bb lean over the second, Bb over the third), CS syncope heritage in the off-beat attack points; the line rises to C6 with the round's widest ring then falls G-F as it hands the top register to the flute's coming climb
the Hare STILL RUNNING: R.subject bars 1-2 (Bb form, the lab-verified augmentation pairing) off the beat over the bell tones; the flute then breathes two bars, handing the tune on mid-statement

the WIDEST SPREAD opens and the Hare hits its PANIC GEAR: the wiggle rebuilt a fifth higher with sixteenth darts, then off a half-bar breath a sixteenth scramble F-G-A-Bb springs the dodge leap Bb5->F6 onto the bar-92 downbeat - the piece's highest note, held, above the bar-69 D6 crest - and the running close lands the union Bb WITH the augmented Hound's final bell

the augmented Hound doubled at the octave below; the doubling DROPS for the final Bb (bar 93 second half) so no sustained low Bb sounds before the reserved bar-96 seal, and the breath sets up the cadence call

H.subject COMPLETE in augmentation, all four bars at double values: the same tune at double length, bell tones, nothing decorated - the proclamation the whole piece aims at

head-call with the horn, dominant root drive, then the RESERVED LOW Bb SPENT: Bb1 for the first time in the piece, the root fall F2->Bb1 sealing the cadence at the very bottom; the dominant drive is snapped calls out of gaps with the horn, converging in eighths only at the last dotted beat

answer-call bending 5->1 downward (the tonal-answer gesture compressed), then the SEVENTH of the V7 flashed in the trio's gap-answers - the one dissonant tone in the exchange, licensed cadential pressure - falling into the octave Bb

the answered call (1->5 on the dominant), the V7 drive ANSWERED out of the low pair's gap at the reclaimed F6 (snap, silence, snap), and the final spring F6->Bb6: the last note is the highest note of the piece, flute at the top of the bare octaves

V.final_cadence rest-accented: the horn throws the Hound head-call one last time (the voice that opened the piece calls it home), then the V7 drive becomes an EXCHANGE - low pair snaps the leading tone out of the bar-95 downbeat, falls silent while the trio answers, snaps again, and only the last dotted beat converges in eighths before the lift into the seal
answer-call rising 3->1 in the dominant, then the fifth of the V7 spoken in the trio's gap-answers, stepping C6->Bb5 into the seal
